sentences.text: Then I'm gonna, Mama, I'm going to say good-bye to you.
He took out-- in our house, my father took in our-- the minyan.
This meant the-- the scroll and he took care.
He was the-- you know, like a-- like a rabbi, like a cantor.
He-- he's conducting the-- the prayers.
And when-- and when this happened, when they bombed, he took out the scroll and he run to save the-- the scroll and save himself.
We have to move to Maniewicze, because all the houses were-- was burned.
And in Maniewicze, we-- we find a place to live.
And we stay and the Nazis came.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, we live in fear.
He went to the forest because he heard they're gonna take to work men.
So he said, they're not gonna take me, any event.
And a good Ukraine person gave him a rifle and two hand grenades and bullets.
And-- and he has a connection.
Jewish people in Ukraine are in the forest.
And we stayed there for two year-- for two years in the forest.
I knew that he is somewhere alive.

And I mean, I was-- two young people came to find out if someone is alive, and they told me that my husband is-- is in-- in the group, partisans, and then they-- they take me along and I went with this-- with this young man to-- to the forest.
The-- the ghetto was very short, very short.
Because the, you know, the Nazis saw that the people during-- during the ghetto, during the gathering and a lot of people run out.
So they-- they surround us all around and put us in one ghetto.
Was no surrounding with the-- with the fence.
People and one Nazi and that's all.
1941, They took all the men away.
And then the women-- the women and children and old-- in the--
In 1941, my father was hidden in a garage.
They took us out, and they put us in the middle of the street.
And they counted all the people, and they took us over to our-- this Maniewicze was divided in the middle.
This city, Maniewicze was the railroad.
So they took us from one place to the other place and put us in a ghetto.
And this was on a Friday.
And-- and on a Saturday, they took out everybody to-- to kill.
Oh, I went-- in-- in this ghetto, I went to visit my cousin and-- and his family.
They live in this-- this place in the ghetto where they put us in this-- in this ghetto, in this street.
So I visit my cousin, and they-- they are not in the house.
And I-- and I went into a barn and I was sitting in this barn.
All of a sudden, I saw my cousin came out from this hiding place, and she went into the house.
So that time, I came out and I says, where are you hiding?
So she took me on-- on the-- on the attic of-- of this house.
And then in the next morning, we-- we went down, was quiet and we crawl-- we-- through the railroad and we went in the forest.
And there was one Polish person whose name was Slowek.
He took in all who survived, who run out.
He guard the forest, a private forest, and he took all the people to this forest, and he took care on us.
And he was a Polish person.
And then my-- in this forest, I told you before that two or three boys came to find out what-- what happened.
And-- and-- and I went to them, and I met my husband in the-- in the forest with the partisans.
There was a connection with the Ukrainian people.
Some of them, they're anti-Semite and some not.
Then-- then we have more-- you know, the Russian army, which they cannot go through the borderline, they went into the forest.
So we have a Russian headquarters.
We have groups every 10 kilometer a group, not together.
You know, in case the general come-- one group to, you know, to kill or to surround us.
So that's why they divided in groups the people. 10 kilometer a group, 100 people or 50 people in that.
There was a group, men, which they are fighters and the civilian was separated.
That-- our commander was a Ukraine.
He was a Communist during the Polish regime.
So when the war started, he run out.

He came to Kiev and in Kiev, they gathered all more together the army and more and-- and they send him back, you know, the-- the officers, you know, the general or-- They said-- they said you have to go back to your town and organize our partisans in the forest.
And this you can help us underground to do underground work.
He take good care of everybody.
Children were in-- in civilian camp, separate.
Responsibilities for the-- for the men?
From beginning, they went at night burn bridges, destroyed the electricity.
And after a while, the headquarters send in a-- a very important person who knew how to make bombs.
So when the war started, this man, his name was-- I forgot.
So when the war started, he was working with the cannons.
So he hid the big bullets in the ground, this man.
And when-- when he came to us, he says, we'll go, we'll dig out those bullets, we'll bring them to the forest, and we'll try to make bomb.
What they did, they put-- unscrew the capsule, put them in kettles, in water, put fire under till the toll melted.

You know, the toll-- the-- the-- And when this was melted, they made little boxes-- wooden boxes and attach with all kind, you know, batteries and-- and they put this toll in these boxes and this was the-- the bomb.
And with those bomb, they went on the railroad at night, under the tracks, they put under the tracks.
And when the-- the train from Germany went every hour on the hour with ammunition, with soldiers, with food. and-- and the partisan put those bomb under the tracks, and-- and they explode.
I told you from beginning, we have to go at night and find food.
And then after a while, the Ukrainian people support us.
And one night I saw two airplanes threw a lot of things like clothes, some kind of food, you know, the Communist.
Potatoes in the morning, potatoes at night.
Twice a day I cook potatoes.
We didn't-- from beginning, just potatoes, but no salt.
Potatoes and water and that's all.
From beginning, we-- we make fires and bake potatoes.
We didn't have bread, just potatoes.
We made the-- yes, from logs.
You put like this and this-- and this was our-- they called the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, tent.
There was groups-- a lot of groups and all kind commanders, Russians.
And we have the headquarters, you know, they used to come every evening to tell us what is going on on the front.
This is to take care of the guards.
Some people, you know, to change guards every two hours.
You know, and this group has to wash, the young ladies, they wash clothes and was divided.
It was not too cold in the forest.
From beginning we made fire outside and-- and around the fire.
And then we have the-- those tents and-- and we find by Ukraine people those iron [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wood.
For the-- for the cold weather, we-- we were not afraid.
We're afraid only for the Nazis.
The commander gave us the names.
By the-- because our commander, he had a diary.
And this diary he made, they took us to Moscow.
And-- and then my cousin find this book in Russian language.
He translate it into Hebrew and we translated into English.
And-- and my son, Jack, he's a writer.
So he wrote a lot of books.
They find it in-- in Moscow.
I think this was found already after the war.
After war, you're on you're own.
So we went to the bigger cities.
And there we find an apartment, one-room apartment.
But was-- our houses was bombed.
They bombed the city for one-- for one year.
And they-- they-- it's hard to-- was hard to find a place to live.
So we find one-room apartment in Rowne.
So my son, Jack, was born in Rowne.
I was pregnant in-- under the bombs all the time, the nine months.
And-- and when the time came to-- [INAUDIBLE] to have my baby, my husband put me in a carriage, a horse and wagon.
He took me to a hospital.
There was no heat and no light.
And I survived with the baby.
So and-- what-- what else I want to tell you?
And this one-room apartment, we took in two orphans.
The father was killed in-- in the army, in the war, during the war.
The mother was freeze to death in the forest.
She went to look for food and she couldn't-- she got lost.
So we took on those two children, a boy and a girl, sister and brother, and they stayed with us till we came up to Russia.
Nagana, ?] they gathered all the orphaned and they went to Israel.
With the baby, we start-- you know, we told you.
We plan to go to Palestine.
And we couldn't go through because they didn't give us certificate to go through.
Bindermichl was a camp, displace camp.
And there was one of the officers, he was checking, you know, visitors camp.
He met my husband, and he says-- start asking him questions, from where you come, where you-- what's your plans.
He says, my plan's to go only to Palestine.
And ask him if he have any relatives in America.
I have a brother in Chicago.
He said, give me the name of your brother and your name and the city and-- and your brother will find you.
He said my-- his father-in-law works at the [?
Fulwitz ?] in New York, and I'll put us in the [?
And after two weeks, he sent in papers with the picture of my husband and-- and the baby and everything.
So his brother find us through the papers.
Right away he send us papers to fill out and-- and we refused to-- to go-- to come to America.
Just into-- to go to Palestine, we didn't have the certificate.
So the-- the brother-in-law says, come to America.
From America, you can go to Palestine.
So we listened to him, and we came to America.
From Rowne, we went to Germany, Boynton.
--till I told you that the officer find my husband, and he connected to-- to-- you know, through the [?
No, I didn't-- I didn't feel good.
I was laying more than-- it was-- I-- I got an-- a nervous heart, still up to date.
Before I didn't feel I want to call you not to come.
And we stay overnight in New York, then we came to Chicago by train to our brother-- to my husband's brother and sister-in-law.
And we stayed there, then we couldn't find a room even that time.
It was hard to find an apartment.
So we visit our-- we have relatives in-- in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Chicago?
So we visit Milwaukee, the relatives, and there we start looking for apartment in Milwaukee.
Jack was the time when we traveled, six months old.
In Milwaukee, he started-- he-- he worked for a-- he was a cutter for glove, by hand.
Then he quit this job, was too hard.
He used to come with blisters on his hand.
And then-- then he worked for a shoe company.
